text: MarketReel — Global Film Distribution Intelligence IndiaFilm Censorship & Certification GuidelinesCentral Board of Film Certification (CBFC) — Regulatory Intelligence for Film Distribution1. Regulatory AuthorityThe Central Board of Film Certification (CBFC), colloquially the 'Censor Board', is a statutory body under the Ministry of Information & Broadcasting, Government of India. It certifies films for public exhibition under the Cinematograph Act, 1952 (amended 2023). No film may be publicly exhibited in India without a valid CBFC certificate.2. Certification SystemCertificateFull NameAudienceAgent Decision RuleUUniversalAll ages including childrenApprove as-is for all platforms and demographics. No restrictions.UA 7Parental Guidance 7Children under 7 with parental guidanceApprove; flag mild thematic content. Standard family framing.UA 13Parental Guidance 13Children under 13 with parental guidanceApprove; moderate thematic/violence content present. Teen-appropriate marketing.UA 16Parental Guidance 16Children under 16 with parental guidanceApprove with care; mature themes present. Adult supervision framing.AAdults Only18 years and aboveRestrict all marketing to 18. No family positioning. Theatrical: adults-only screenings.SSpecialist AudiencesSpecific professional audiences onlyNot viable for commercial theatrical release.3. Absolutely Prohibited Content — Automatic RefusalContent that brings the Constitution of India, Parliament, Supreme Court, or the President into contempt.Content that encourages, glorifies, or justifies terrorism, separatism, or violent extremism within Indian territory.Content that promotes or incites hatred between religious communities, castes, or linguistic groups — including dialogue, imagery, or narrative framing.Sexually explicit content of any kind — nudity, simulated sexual acts, or explicit sexual dialogue. India has no theatrical certification pathway for explicit sexual content.Child Sexual Abuse Material (CSAM) — absolute prohibition with criminal liability.Content denigrating women through sexual objectification without redemptive narrative framing, per the Indecent Representation of Women (Prohibition) Act, 1986.Drug use depicted approvingly or glamorously without negative consequence.Harmful superstitious practices depicted as real and effective without critical framing.Mockery or insult of any recognised religion, its deities, or religious practices in a manner likely to cause public disorder.4. Conditional Content — Edit or Disclaimer RequiredContent CategoryDefault CBFC ActionAgent RecommendationKissing / prolonged romantic physical contactOften trimmed, especially prolonged scenesFlag all kissing scenes over 3 seconds. Prepare edited version for UA path.Abusive / profane languageMuted or replaced for UA; removed in UPrepare clean audio dub with expletives removed for UA certification path.Smoking / tobacco useAnti-tobacco scrolling disclaimer on screen during all smoking scenesEnsure post-production statutory disclaimer overlay in all CBFC submissions.Alcohol consumptionDisclaimer required for UA; contextual for AAdd scrolling statutory health warning during all drinking scenes.Violence — realistic or graphicCuts for excessive gore; context-dependent for actionReview all scenes with blood, physical injury, torture. CGI blood acceptable; practical gore may require cut.LGBTQ content (non-explicit)Permissible post-Navtej Singh Johar 2018; contextual reviewRomantic same-sex content legally permissible. Explicit same-sex content: flag for review.Political satire of Indian leadersContext-sensitive; state-level objections possibleConsult local legal counsel if film contains recognisable parody of Indian political figures.Nudity — non-sexualArtistic nudity case-by-case at A-certFull frontal nudity generally not passed. Partial nudity in artistic context: prepare covered edit.Historical or mythological figuresExtremely sensitive — any perceived disrespect triggers protests and bansFlag ANY portrayal of Hindu deities, freedom fighters, or revered figures. Mandatory CBFC pre-consultation.5. Religion & Caste Sensitivity RulesPortrayals of Hindu deities (Rama, Krishna, Durga, Shiva, etc.) in any context other than traditional devotional framing require pre-certification community consultation.Islamic religious practice depicted negatively or in a criminal context: HIGH RISK — likely CBFC refusal and potential civil unrest.Caste-based discrimination depicted graphically: Review required. Some depictions must carry disclaimers.Films involving Hindu-Muslim communal violence or the 1947 Partition: Mandatory sensitivity review. Pre-screen with CBFC advisory panel strongly recommended.Christian missionary activity depicted negatively: MEDIUM RISK in Goa, Kerala, Northeast India.6. State-Level Override RiskEven after a national CBFC certificate is issued, individual Indian states can ban theatrical exhibition under Section 6 of the Cinematograph Act. Key risk states:Uttar Pradesh, Maharashtra, Rajasthan: High propensity for state bans on content deemed to insult Hindu religious sentiment.Tamil Nadu: Sensitive to films depicting Tamil cultural or linguistic identity negatively.Punjab: Sensitive to Khalistan, Operation Blue Star, or Sikh identity content.AGENT RULEEven a CBFC-certified film can be blocked in 1–5 states. Assess state-level risk separately from national CBFC certification risk. Budget for state-specific legal mitigation.7. OTT / Streaming RulesOTT platforms governed by IT (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021.Platforms must self-classify content into: U, U/A 7, U/A 13, U/A 16, A.OTT content does NOT require CBFC certification — voluntary self-classification applies.Government retains takedown authority. Grievance redressal mechanism mandatory.8. Submission TimelineSubmit application and film to relevant CBFC regional office (Mumbai, Delhi, Chennai, Kolkata, Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Trivandrum, Cuttack, Guwahati).Examination Committee screens the film (typically 2–3 members).Certificate issued OR list of required cuts sent within 68 working days (statutory limit).If cuts unacceptable: appeal to Film Certification Appellate Tribunal (FCAT).Final appeal: High Court or Supreme Court.AGENT TIMELINE RULEAllow minimum 10–12 weeks from submission to certified release in India. Do not announce India release date until CBFC certificate is confirmed.9. Agent Decision Framework — IndiaTrigger Detected in FilmRisk LevelRecommended ActionHindu deity mockery or distortionHIGH — BAN RISKDo not release without removing all such content. Consult CBFC advisory panel before submission.Explicit sexual content (any)HIGH — A CERT or CUTSMandatory cuts for UA path. A-certificate theatrical very limited reach.Drug use presented approvinglyMEDIUMAdd consequence scenes and statutory disclaimer. Recut if necessary.Smoking or alcohol scenesLOW — DISCLAIMERPost-production statutory disclaimer overlays. No cut required.LGBTQ romantic content (non-explicit)LOW–MEDIUMLegally permissible. Flag for state-level risk assessment. Proceed with standard submission.Real Indian political figures satirisedMEDIUM–HIGHLegal review mandatory. Consider removing or fictionalising.Communal violence (Hindu-Muslim)HIGHPre-screen with CBFC advisory panel. High state-ban risk.Military or national security depictionMEDIUMConsult Ministry of Defence if Indian military shown. NOC may be required.Pakistani or China conflict framingMEDIUM–HIGHPolitically sensitive. Seek CBFC pre-guidance. Risk of state bans.MarketReel Confidential India Censorship Guidelines For Agent Reasoning & Internal Distribution Use Only
